title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz by L. Frank Baum

informationalTexts.title: France in the 1600s: The Age of Musketeers
informationalTexts.text: In the 1600s, France was ruled by powerful kings. King Louis XIII was on the throne, but much of the real power was held by Cardinal Richelieu, his chief minister. During this time, musketeers were elite soldiers who protected the king. They were skilled fighters who carried muskets (early guns) and swords. The musketeers wore fancy uniforms with blue cloaks decorated with white crosses. They were known for their bravery, loyalty, and honor. Being a musketeer was one of the greatest honors a young man could achieve. The story of The Three Musketeers takes place during this exciting time in French history.
questions: Who was the king of France during the time of the Three Musketeers?
What two weapons did musketeers carry?
What color were the musketeers' cloaks?

informationalTexts.title: Alexandre Dumas: The Master Storyteller
informationalTexts.text: Alexandre Dumas was born in France in 1802. His father was a general in Napoleon's army, and his grandmother was a slave from Haiti. Dumas grew up loving stories and theater. He moved to Paris and worked as a playwright before becoming a famous novelist. He wrote many adventure stories, but The Three Musketeers became his most popular book. Dumas was known for writing exciting plots full of sword fights, romance, and friendship. He often worked with assistants who helped him research historical facts. By the time he died in 1870, he had written over 100 books and plays!
questions: What year was Alexandre Dumas born?
What was his father's job?
How many books and plays did Dumas write in his lifetime?

informationalTexts.title: Sword Fighting: The Art of Dueling
informationalTexts.text: In the 1600s, sword fighting was an important skill for soldiers and noblemen. The most common sword was called a rapier—a long, thin blade perfect for quick attacks. Musketeers trained for hours every day to master fencing. A duel was a formal fight between two people to settle an argument or defend their honor. Duels followed strict rules: opponents would bow, agree on the rules, and fight until one person surrendered or was wounded. Though dueling was often illegal, it continued because honor was so important to people of that time. Good swordsmen were respected and feared.
questions: What was the most common type of sword in the 1600s?
Why did people fight duels?
What would happen at the end of a duel?

informationalTexts.title: Paris in the 1600s: A City of Contrast
informationalTexts.text: Paris in the 1600s was a city of grand palaces and muddy streets. The royal family lived in beautiful buildings with gardens and fountains. Rich nobles wore silk and velvet clothes and attended fancy parties. But most people were poor. They lived in crowded neighborhoods with narrow streets. There was no running water or electricity. People threw their garbage into the streets, which made the city smell terrible. Horse-drawn carriages clattered over cobblestone roads. Despite the poverty, Paris was growing into Europe's center of art, fashion, and culture.
questions: How did rich and poor people's lives differ in Paris?
Why did the city smell bad?
What was Paris becoming known for in Europe?

informationalTexts.title: Cardinal Richelieu: Power Behind the Throne
informationalTexts.text: Cardinal Richelieu was one of the most powerful men in France. He was a Catholic cardinal (a high church official) but also served as King Louis XIII's chief minister. Richelieu was brilliant and ambitious. He wanted to make France the strongest country in Europe. To do this, he increased the king's power and weakened the nobles who opposed him. He also created a network of spies to learn about his enemies. In The Three Musketeers, Richelieu is portrayed as the villain, but in real history, many people saw him as a great leader who made France powerful.
questions: What two important jobs did Richelieu hold?
What was Richelieu's main goal for France?
How did Richelieu keep track of his enemies?

informationalTexts.title: Muskets: The Weapons That Changed Warfare
informationalTexts.text: A musket was an early type of gun that gave musketeers their name. These weapons were about five feet long and very heavy. To fire a musket, a soldier had to pour gunpowder down the barrel, add a lead ball, pack it tight with a rod, then light the powder with a slow-burning match or flint. This process took time, so soldiers could only fire one or two shots per minute. Muskets were powerful but not very accurate. A soldier might miss a target that was only 50 yards away! Because reloading was slow, musketeers also carried swords for close combat.
questions: How long was a typical musket?
How many shots could a soldier fire per minute?
Why did musketeers carry swords as well as muskets?

informationalTexts.title: Honor and Loyalty in the 1600s
informationalTexts.text: In the time of the Three Musketeers, honor was everything. A person's honor meant their reputation and how others saw them. If someone insulted you, you were expected to defend your honor—sometimes by fighting a duel. Loyalty was equally important. Musketeers swore loyalty to their king and to each other. Breaking your word or betraying a friend was considered shameful. The famous motto 'All for one, one for all' shows how important friendship and loyalty were to the musketeers. They would risk their lives for each other without hesitation.
questions: What did 'honor' mean in the 1600s?
How might someone defend their honor?
What is the musketeers' famous motto?

informationalTexts.title: Becoming a Musketeer: Training and Requirements
informationalTexts.text: Becoming a musketeer was not easy. Young men usually came from noble families or had connections to someone important. They started as cadets, training in sword fighting, horseback riding, and shooting. Cadets also had to learn good manners and how to behave at court. The training was intense and could last several years. Only the best fighters and most loyal soldiers were accepted into the musketeers. Once accepted, a musketeer received a uniform, weapons, and a small salary. More importantly, they gained honor, respect, and the friendship of fellow musketeers.
questions: What three skills did cadet musketeers learn?
How long did training typically last?
What did a new musketeer receive when accepted?

informationalTexts.title: Women in 17th Century France
informationalTexts.text: Life for women in 1600s France depended on their social class. Poor women worked hard as servants, farmers, or shopkeepers. They had few rights and little education. Rich women, called ladies, had more freedom but were still expected to marry and obey their fathers and husbands. Some noble women, however, were powerful and clever. They ran salons (gatherings where people discussed ideas), influenced politics, and sometimes even plotted against their enemies. In The Three Musketeers, characters like Milady show how some women used intelligence and charm to gain power in a world controlled by men.
questions: How did poor and rich women's lives differ?
What were salons?
How did some women gain power despite having few rights?

informationalTexts.title: The Royal Court: Where Power and Intrigue Meet
informationalTexts.text: The royal court was where the king, queen, and nobles gathered. It was held in beautiful palaces with paintings, tapestries, and gold decorations everywhere. Court life involved fancy balls, elaborate meals, and careful etiquette (rules of polite behavior). But the court was also dangerous. People schemed and plotted to gain the king's favor. Spies listened to conversations. Rivals spread rumors to destroy each other's reputations. One wrong word could mean disgrace or exile. In The Three Musketeers, much of the drama happens at court, where political games are just as dangerous as sword fights.
questions: What kinds of events happened at the royal court?
Why was the court considered dangerous?
What could happen if someone made a mistake at court?

informationalTexts.title: Horses: Essential Transportation
informationalTexts.text: In the 1600s, horses were the fastest way to travel and essential for musketeers. Good horses were expensive and highly valued. Musketeers needed strong, fast horses for battle and travel. A soldier might spend months' wages on a quality horse. Horses required constant care—feeding, grooming, and shoeing. Long journeys on horseback were exhausting. A rider might travel 30-50 miles in a day, but this left both horse and rider tired and sore. In The Three Musketeers, d'Artagnan's journey from Gascony to Paris takes many days on horseback.
questions: Why were horses so important to musketeers?
How far could a rider travel in one day?
Why were good horses expensive?

informationalTexts.title: Gascon Pride: D'Artagnan's Heritage
informationalTexts.text: D'Artagnan comes from Gascony, a region in southwest France. Gascons had a reputation for being brave, proud, and quick to anger. They were known as excellent fighters who never backed down from a challenge. Many Gascons were poor but noble, meaning they came from good families but had little money. This made them eager to prove themselves and earn honor through courage and skill. Gascons often spoke with a distinctive accent and were sometimes mocked by people from other regions. D'Artagnan's Gascon pride drives many of his actions in the story.
questions: What region of France does d'Artagnan come from?
What three traits were Gascons known for?
Why were many Gascons eager to prove themselves?

informationalTexts.title: The Queen's Diamonds: A True Historical Mystery
informationalTexts.text: Queen Anne of Austria was the real queen of France during this time. She was Spanish, not French, which made some people distrust her. There really was a scandal involving diamond studs (decorative pins). King Louis XIII gave twelve diamond studs to the queen as a gift. Cardinal Richelieu, who disliked the queen, discovered she had given some of them to an English duke she was friends with. This created a political crisis because England and France were enemies. While Dumas changed some facts for his story, the basic incident really happened and caused great embarrassment at court.
questions: Where was Queen Anne originally from?
What gift did the king give the queen?
Why did giving the diamonds away cause a scandal?

informationalTexts.title: Letter Writing: Communication in the 1600s
informationalTexts.text: In the 1600s, there was no email, phones, or text messaging. People communicated through letters written with quill pens dipped in ink. Letters were folded, sealed with wax, and stamped with a personal seal. Sending a letter was slow and expensive. A messenger might take days or weeks to deliver a letter, depending on the distance. Important letters were often sent with trusted servants. People had to be careful what they wrote because letters could be intercepted and read by enemies. In The Three Musketeers, secret letters play a crucial role in the plot.
questions: What tools did people use to write letters?
How long might it take to deliver a letter?
Why did people have to be careful about what they wrote?

informationalTexts.title: The Siege of La Rochelle
informationalTexts.text: La Rochelle was a coastal city in France where many Protestants lived. In the 1620s, there was conflict between Catholics and Protestants in France. Cardinal Richelieu led the king's army to besiege La Rochelle (surround it and cut off supplies). The siege lasted over a year. The city was surrounded by land and sea, preventing food and supplies from getting in. Thousands of people inside La Rochelle starved. Finally, the city surrendered in 1628. This real historical event appears in The Three Musketeers as the musketeers join the siege. It was one of the most important military events of that time.
questions: Why was La Rochelle under siege?
How long did the siege last?
How did the siege end?

informationalTexts.title: Food and Drink in 17th Century France
informationalTexts.text: Food in the 1600s was very different from today. Rich people ate elaborate meals with many courses: soup, roasted meat, pies, vegetables, and sweets. They drank wine at almost every meal because water was often unsafe to drink. Spices like cinnamon and nutmeg were expensive and showed wealth. Poor people ate simpler food: bread, cheese, porridge, and occasionally meat. There was no refrigeration, so food spoiled quickly. People preserved meat by salting or smoking it. In The Three Musketeers, the characters often meet in taverns where they eat, drink, and make plans.
questions: Why did people drink wine instead of water?
How did people keep meat from spoiling?
What did poor people typically eat?

informationalTexts.title: The Real D'Artagnan
informationalTexts.text: D'Artagnan was a real person! His full name was Charles de Batz de Castelmore d'Artagnan. He was born around 1611 in Gascony, just like in the book. The real d'Artagnan did join the musketeers and became their captain. He served King Louis XIV (the son of Louis XIII) for many years. D'Artagnan was known for being brave, loyal, and skilled in battle. He died in 1673 while leading troops in a war. Dumas based his character on this real musketeer but changed many details and added adventures that never really happened.
questions: Was d'Artagnan a real person or completely fictional?
What position did the real d'Artagnan eventually hold?
How did the real d'Artagnan die?

informationalTexts.title: Athos, Porthos, and Aramis: Fact and Fiction
informationalTexts.text: The Three Musketeers were based on real people too, though Dumas changed their names and personalities. The real musketeers were Armand de Sillègue d'Athos d'Autevielle, Isaac de Porthau, and Henri d'Aramitz. These three men really were friends and served as musketeers together. However, Dumas invented most of their adventures and personalities. The real Athos was not a mysterious nobleman in hiding. The real Porthos was not obsessed with wealth and strength. And the real Aramis did not dream of becoming a priest. Dumas took their names and created memorable characters for his story.
questions: Were the Three Musketeers based on real people?
Did Dumas use their real names?
Which parts of the musketeers did Dumas invent?

informationalTexts.title: Fashion in the Age of Musketeers
informationalTexts.text: Fashion in the 1600s was elaborate and expensive. Men wore puffy breeches (short pants), long stockings, and fancy jackets with many buttons. They also wore large white collars, feathered hats, and high leather boots with folded tops. Rich men's clothes were made of silk and velvet in bright colors. Swords were not just weapons but fashion accessories. Women wore long dresses with tight bodices and full skirts. They used lots of makeup and wore their hair in fancy styles with ribbons and jewels. Looking fashionable showed wealth and social status.
questions: What did men wear on their heads?
Why did people dress so elaborately?
What made someone's clothes expensive?

informationalTexts.title: Medicine in the 1600s
informationalTexts.text: Medical care in the 1600s was primitive and often dangerous. Doctors did not understand germs or how diseases spread. They believed illness came from an imbalance of four 'humors' (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile). Common treatments included bloodletting (cutting someone to let blood out), purging (giving medicines to make people vomit), and using leeches. Many medicines were made from herbs, some helpful and some poisonous. Surgery was performed without anesthesia or antibiotics. A simple wound could easily become infected and kill someone. In The Three Musketeers, injuries from sword fights and gunshots were often fatal.
questions: What did doctors believe caused illness?
What were three common medical treatments?
Why were sword wounds so dangerous?

informationalTexts.title: The Diamond Studs: Symbols of Power
informationalTexts.text: Diamonds in the 1600s were extremely rare and valuable. They came from far-away India and were difficult to cut and polish with the tools available then. Only the richest nobles and royalty could afford them. Owning diamonds showed wealth and power. The king giving diamond studs to the queen was a significant political and personal gesture. Each stud would have been worth a fortune—enough to buy a house or a herd of cattle. Losing or giving away such valuable gifts was not just careless; it could be seen as an insult to the giver and a political statement.
questions: Where did diamonds come from in the 1600s?
Who could afford to own diamonds?
Why was losing the diamond studs so serious?

informationalTexts.title: England vs. France: Old Enemies
informationalTexts.text: England and France had been enemies for centuries. They fought many wars over land, power, and religion. In the 1600s, tensions were high. England was Protestant while France was Catholic, adding religious conflict to political rivalry. The English Channel separated the two countries, but their ships often clashed at sea. Spies from both countries tried to learn each other's secrets. In The Three Musketeers, the Duke of Buckingham represents England's power and its complicated relationship with France. Any friendship between a French queen and an English duke was considered treason.
questions: How long had England and France been enemies?
What two things did England and France fight about?
Why was the queen's friendship with an English duke dangerous?

informationalTexts.title: Taverns and Inns: Meeting Places of Adventure
informationalTexts.text: Taverns and inns were important gathering places in the 1600s. Travelers stopped at inns to rest, eat, and stable their horses. Taverns served food and drink, mostly wine and beer. These places were noisy and crowded, with people from all walks of life. Merchants made deals, soldiers played cards, and locals gossiped about news. Taverns were also places where fights often broke out. In The Three Musketeers, important meetings happen at taverns like the inn where d'Artagnan first meets the musketeers' enemies. Innkeepers often heard valuable information and knew everyone's business.
questions: What services did inns provide?
Who might you meet at a tavern?
Why were taverns important for spreading news and information?

informationalTexts.title: Friendship: The Heart of the Story
informationalTexts.text: At its core, The Three Musketeers is a story about friendship. D'Artagnan arrives in Paris as a young man with no friends or money. Through courage and loyalty, he earns the friendship of three musketeers. Together, they face dangers that none could overcome alone. The motto 'All for one, one for all' means they will always help each other, no matter the cost. This kind of loyalty was highly valued in the 1600s and remains important today. The musketeers trust each other with their lives. Their friendship is stronger than the wealth, power, and scheming of their enemies.
questions: What is the main theme of The Three Musketeers?
What does 'All for one, one for all' mean?
How does d'Artagnan gain his friends?

word1.definition: A large, flat area of grassland with few trees.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 1: "prairie" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A powerful spinning windstorm that can cause great destruction.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 1: "cyclone" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Very unusual or remarkable; beyond what is ordinary or common.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 2: "extraordinary" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A woman who practices magic or witchcraft.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 2: "sorceress" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Boring, annoying, or making you feel tired and impatient.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 3: "tiresome" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A friend or someone who travels or spends time with you.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 3: "companion" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Dark, dim, and depressing; causing a sad or worried feeling.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 4: "gloomy" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Small plants, bushes, and shrubs growing beneath tall trees in a forest.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 4: "undergrowth" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Put under a magic spell; also means filled with delight and wonder.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 5: "enchanted" is used in the story.

word2.definition: In a gentle, caring, and loving way.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 5: "tenderly" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Bravery; the ability to do something even when you are afraid.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 6: "courage" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A statement or situation where two things oppose or disagree with each other.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 6: "contradiction" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: A friend or companion, especially someone who shares your struggles or adventures.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 7: "comrade" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Something that blocks your path or makes it difficult to move forward.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 7: "obstacle" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Containing substances that can cause illness, injury, or death.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 8: "poisonous" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Feeling hopeless and willing to do anything to change a bad situation.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 8: "desperate" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Something you owe to someone, often because they helped you.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 9: "debt" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A plan or clever idea to accomplish something.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 9: "scheme" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Extremely bright and impressive; so brilliant it's hard to look at.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 10: "dazzling" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Eyeglasses worn to help people see better or protect their eyes.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 10: "spectacles" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Causing fear or dread; extremely bad or frightening.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 11: "terrible" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A private room, especially a bedroom or meeting room.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 11: "chamber" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: The land or area controlled by a ruler or government.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 12: "domain" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Clever in a sneaky or deceptive way; skilled at tricking others.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 12: "cunning" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: A person who is trapped or held prisoner.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 13: "captive" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Boldly refusing to obey or showing resistance to authority.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 13: "defiant" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Melted away or disappeared completely, especially in liquid.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 14: "dissolved" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Set free from captivity, control, or oppression.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 14: "liberated" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Tied up or held by a promise or obligation.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 15: "bound" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Having a feeling that something is wrong or that someone is not trustworthy.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 15: "suspicious" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: A person who deceives or tricks others by pretending to be something they are not.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 16: "humbug" is used in the story.

word2.definition: The skill of making your voice seem to come from somewhere else, like a dummy or puppet.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 16: "ventriloquism" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Something that represents or stands for something else, often an idea or quality.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 17: "symbol" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A strong belief in yourself or trust in someone or something.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 17: "confidence" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: To fill with air or gas, causing something to expand and become larger.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 18: "inflate" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Disappeared suddenly and completely.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 18: "vanished" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Wild and not controlled or domesticated by humans.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 19: "untamed" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Pulled back suddenly in fear, disgust, or pain.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 19: "recoiled" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Easily broken, damaged, or destroyed; delicate.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 20: "fragile" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Small, delicate, and pretty; refined or elegant.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 20: "dainty" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: Serious and sincere; showing genuine effort and determination.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 21: "earnest" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Shining brightly with light, happiness, or beauty.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 21: "radiant" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: The future that is meant to happen; what is fated or predetermined.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 22: "destiny" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A goodbye; a parting wish for someone leaving.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 22: "farewell" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: To make something known that was previously hidden or secret.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 23: "reveal" is used in the story.

word2.definition: Something that brings both happiness and sadness at the same time.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 23: "bittersweet" appears in this chapter.

word1.definition: To hug someone warmly; also to accept or welcome something willingly.
word1.sentence: From Chapter 24: "embrace" is used in the story.

word2.definition: A feeling of peaceful happiness and satisfaction with what you have.
word2.sentence: From Chapter 24: "contentment" appears in this chapter.

questions.question: What is Dorothy's life like in Kansas before the cyclone?
options: Gray, flat, and quiet — Uncle Henry and Aunt Em rarely smile or laugh
She lives in a big city with many friends
She goes to school and plays sports

questions.question: The author describes Kansas as gray over and over. Why do you think he does this? What might he be setting up for later in the story?
What happened to the Wicked Witch of the East?
options: She flew away on a broomstick
Dorothy's house landed on her and destroyed her
The Witch of the North defeated her in a battle

questions.question: Dorothy wants to go home to gray, dull Kansas instead of staying in this beautiful, colorful land. Why? What does this tell you about what 'home' means to her?
What does the Scarecrow want more than anything?
The Scarecrow says he has no brains, but he talks cleverly and asks thoughtful questions. Find one thing the Scarecrow says or does that makes you think he might already be smarter than he realizes.
How does the Scarecrow handle falling down on the rough road?
options: He cries and wants to give up
He picks himself up without complaining and keeps going every time
He asks the Tin Woodman to carry him

questions.question: The forest is described as dark, gloomy, and full of strange sounds. How does this setting create a mood? How does it make you feel as a reader, and why do you think the author puts this scene right before they find the Tin Woodman?
What does the Tin Woodman want the Wizard to give him?

The Tin Woodman says he cannot feel anything because he has no heart. But he cries when he is sad. Does that seem strange to you? What might the author be trying to show us about what the Tin Woodman already has?
What pattern has the author created with the main characters?
options: They each want something they believe they do not have
They are all afraid of the Wizard
They all want to stay in Oz forever

questions.question: Dorothy slaps a lion on the nose to protect Toto. The Scarecrow figures out the safest path. The Tin Woodman moves a beetle off the road so it won't be stepped on. What do these actions tell us about whether the characters already have what they wish for?
Who comes up with the plan to cross the great ditch?

The Scarecrow solves problems, the Lion carries everyone across danger, and the Tin Woodman protects the group. Each one uses the exact quality they say they are missing. What do you think the author is trying to show us?
Why aren't the Scarecrow and Tin Woodman affected by the poisonous poppies?
options: They are not made of flesh and do not breathe
They ran through the field too quickly

questions.question: The poppies are beautiful but deadly — they look safe but are actually dangerous. How does this connect to the bigger theme of the story that things are not always what they seem?
How is the Lion rescued from the poppy field?
options: He wakes up on his own
The Wizard comes to save him
Thousands of field mice pull him out on a cart the Tin Woodman built

questions.question: The Tin Woodman saved the mouse queen without thinking — he simply could not stand to see a creature harmed. What does this automatic kindness tell us about whether he truly has no heart?
What must everyone wear inside the Emerald City?
options: Green spectacles locked onto their heads with a key

questions.question: The Scarecrow wonders: if everyone wears green glasses, how do they know the city is actually green? What do you think — is the city really green, or does it just LOOK green? What might the author be saying about appearances?
The Wizard appears as four different forms to four different visitors. What does he look like to Dorothy?
The Wizard demands that Dorothy kill the Wicked Witch before he will help her. Do you think this is fair? Why do you think the Wizard makes this demand instead of simply helping her?
Which character defeats the Witch's pack of wolves?

Each of Dorothy's friends defeats one of the Witch's attacks using exactly the quality they say they lack: the Tin Woodman uses tireless strength (heart-driven loyalty), the Scarecrow uses cleverness. What does this pattern tell you about what they already possess?
Why is the Wicked Witch afraid of Dorothy?
options: Dorothy is physically stronger than her
Dorothy wears the silver shoes and bears the mark of the Witch of the North's kiss

questions.question: Even as a prisoner, Dorothy sneaks food to the Lion and refuses to give up. Where do you think her strength comes from? Is Dorothy brave in the same way as the Lion, or is her courage different?
How is the Wicked Witch destroyed?
options: Dorothy fights her with a sword
Water accidentally splashes on her and she melts
The Wizard sends a spell to destroy her

questions.question: The most powerful villain in the story is destroyed by an accident — a bucket of water. Dorothy did not plan it or mean to do it. What might the author be saying about the nature of wicked power? Is it as strong as it seems?
Why were the Winged Monkeys forced to obey the Golden Cap?
options: They were evil creatures who enjoyed serving witches
They were punished for a prank and bound by magic to obey whoever wore the cap
The Wizard created them as servants

questions.question: The monkey king tells Dorothy that the great Wizard of Oz was AFRAID of the Winged Monkeys and ran away from them. What does this detail add to your growing suspicions about the Wizard?
Who is the Wizard of Oz really?
options: A powerful sorcerer from another dimension
A small, ordinary man from Omaha, Nebraska who arrived by balloon
A Munchkin who learned dark magic

questions.question: The Wizard says he was too afraid to tell the truth, so he hid behind tricks for years. Do you feel sympathy for him, anger toward him, or both? Explain your feelings using details from the chapter.
What does the Wizard give the Scarecrow as 'brains'?
options: Bran mixed with pins and needles

questions.question: The brains are bran, the heart is silk, and the courage is colored water. None of them are real. Yet the characters feel genuinely changed. How can a symbol work even when it is not magic? What really changed — the gifts, or the characters' belief in themselves?
Why does the balloon leave without Dorothy?
options: The Wizard leaves her behind on purpose
Toto chases a cat, Dorothy runs after him, and the ropes break before she can get back
The balloon is too small for two people
Dorothy decides she does not want to leave

questions.question: Dorothy's hope of getting home disappears with the balloon. But her friends immediately gather around her and promise to find another way. What does this moment tell you about the value of friendship when things go wrong?
How does the group get past the fighting trees?
options: The Lion scares them with a roar
Dorothy uses magic from the silver shoes
The Tin Woodman chops their branches with his axe until they pull back

questions.question: The Scarecrow says: 'Brains, heart, and courage — we have everything we need between us.' How has the group changed since the beginning of the story? Do they still need the Wizard, or have they become their own solution?
Why must the travelers be extremely careful in the china country?
options: The people and buildings are made of fragile china that shatters if touched

questions.question: The china princess says: 'The most important things in life — kindness, beauty, gentleness — are also the most breakable.' Do you agree? What fragile but important things exist in real life that must be treated with care?
How is Glinda different from the Wizard of Oz?
options: Glinda is honest, open, and genuinely powerful — no tricks, no locked doors, no booming voice
Glinda is more frightening than the Wizard

questions.question: Compare Glinda to the Wizard. The Wizard hid behind tricks and illusions. Glinda sits in an open room, speaks honestly, and uses real power. What does this contrast teach you about real strength vs. the appearance of strength?
What does Glinda plan to do with the Golden Cap after Dorothy uses it?
options: Use the final command to set the Winged Monkeys free forever

questions.question: Each of Dorothy's friends receives a role that perfectly matches who they already are: the Scarecrow rules (brains), the Tin Woodman serves with kindness (heart), and the Lion protects (courage). Were these destinies created by the Wizard's gifts, or were they always who these characters were meant to be?
What is the secret of the silver shoes?
options: They can carry the wearer anywhere in the world in three steps
They give the wearer magical powers

questions.question: Dorothy could have gone home from the very first day. If you were Dorothy, would you be upset that no one told you sooner? Or would you agree with Glinda that the journey was necessary? Explain your thinking.
What happens to the silver shoes during Dorothy's flight home?
options: She gives them to Aunt Em
They are lost somewhere in the sky between Oz and Kansas

questions.question: The last line of Dorothy's story is that she feels contentment — not excitement, not triumph, just a quiet feeling that she has everything she needs. Why is contentment the right ending for this story? What has Dorothy learned about what truly matters?

grammar.topic: Relative Pronouns (who, whose, whom, which, that)
grammar.lesson: Relative pronouns connect clauses to nouns. Example: 'The musketeer who fought bravely was d'Artagnan.' Practice identifying and using relative pronouns in sentences about the story.
Progressive tenses show ongoing action. Present progressive: am/is/are + verb+ing. Example: 'The musketeers are riding to Paris.' Practice changing simple present to present progressive.
Modal verbs express possibility, permission, or necessity. Example: 'D'Artagnan must prove his courage.' Practice using different modal verbs to change sentence meaning.
Prepositional phrases begin with prepositions (in, on, at, by, with, etc.) and modify nouns or verbs. Example: 'The sword in his hand gleamed.' Circle prepositional phrases in sentences.
Subordinating conjunctions (because, although, since, while, if) connect dependent and independent clauses. Example: 'Although he was young, d'Artagnan was brave.' Practice combining sentences.
Relative adverbs connect clauses and show place, time, or reason. Example: 'Paris is the place where musketeers train.' Practice using where, when, and why in sentences.
Past progressive shows action that was ongoing in the past: was/were + verb+ing. Example: 'The musketeers were planning their mission.' Practice using past progressive tense.

A fragment is an incomplete sentence. It's missing a subject, verb, or complete thought. Example: 'During the sword fight.' (fragment) → 'D'Artagnan practiced during the sword fight.' (complete). Fix fragments.

A run-on sentence joins two independent clauses without proper punctuation. Fix with a period, comma+conjunction, or semicolon. Example: 'Athos is brave Porthos is strong' → 'Athos is brave, and Porthos is strong.'
Multiple adjectives follow a specific order: opinion, size, age, shape, color, origin, material, purpose. Example: 'beautiful large old French silk cape.' Practice ordering adjectives correctly.
Use a comma after a dependent clause that starts a sentence. Example: 'When the musketeers arrived, they drew their swords.' No comma if the dependent clause comes second.

Active voice: subject does the action ('D'Artagnan challenged the count'). Passive voice: subject receives the action ('The count was challenged by d'Artagnan'). Practice identifying and using both voices.

language.lesson: Authors often define unfamiliar words right in the sentence. Example: 'A duel, or formal fight, was arranged.' The phrase 'formal fight' defines duel. Find context clues in sentences.
These prefixes mean 'not' or 'opposite of.' unhappy = not happy, dishonest = not honest, nonfiction = not fiction. Add these prefixes to base words and explain new meanings.
-ful = full of (fearful), -less = without (fearless), -ness = state of being (braveness). Add suffixes to base words and use them in sentences about the musketeers.
Synonyms have similar meanings (brave/courageous). Antonyms have opposite meanings (brave/cowardly). Practice finding synonyms and antonyms for words from the story.
Some words have more than one meaning. 'Court' can mean a royal palace or a place for trials. Use context to determine meaning. Practice with words that have multiple meanings.
Similes compare two things using 'like' or 'as.' Example: 'D'Artagnan was as quick as lightning.' 'Athos fought like a lion.' Create similes describing the musketeers.
Metaphors compare two things without using 'like' or 'as.' They say something IS something else. Example: 'Porthos is a mountain of strength.' Create metaphors for story characters.
Idioms are expressions that mean something different from the literal words. 'All for one, one for all' means everyone helps everyone. 'Cross swords' means to fight or argue. Explain common idioms.

language.topic: Greek and Latin Roots: port, dict, scrib
language.lesson: port = carry (transport), dict = say (dictate, predict), scrib/script = write (inscribe, manuscript). Find words with these roots and connect to their meanings.
Homophones sound alike but have different spellings and meanings: their/there/they're, to/too/two, knight/night. Choose the correct homophone for sentences about the story.

Denotation is a word's dictionary definition. Connotation is its emotional feeling. 'Slender' and 'scrawny' both mean thin, but slender sounds positive while scrawny sounds negative. Compare word pairs.
Academic words help us talk about learning. Analyze = examine carefully, Evaluate = judge quality, Support = back up with evidence. Use these words to discuss the story.

writing.prompt: Opinion: If you could be d'Artagnan, Athos, Porthos, or Aramis, which musketeer would you choose to be? Explain your choice with at least three reasons. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Write a story about your first day of sword-fighting practice. Include dialogue, sensory details, and a beginning, middle, and end. (4-5 paragraphs)

Informational: Explain how someone in the 1600s would travel from one city to another. Include details about transportation, time, and challenges. Use facts from the informational texts. (3-4 paragraphs)
Opinion: Is it ever right to fight a duel to defend your honor? Explain your position with reasons and examples. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Imagine you intercepted a secret letter. Write the letter (from one character to another) and then write what happens when it's discovered. (4-5 paragraphs total)
Informational: Compare and contrast life for rich and poor people in 1600s France. Include at least three differences. (3-4 paragraphs)
Opinion: Would you rather be a musketeer or a cardinal's guard? Give at least three reasons for your choice. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Write about a time you and your friends worked together to solve a problem. Include dialogue and describe what each person did. (4-5 paragraphs)
Informational: Describe three ways communication in the 1600s was different from today. Explain the advantages and disadvantages of each era. (3-4 paragraphs)
Opinion: Which is more important: courage or loyalty? Explain your answer with examples from the story and from real life. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Imagine you found the missing diamond studs. Write a story about your adventure returning them to the queen. (4-5 paragraphs)
Informational: Explain what the motto 'All for one, one for all' means and why it was important to the musketeers. Give examples of how they lived by this motto. (3-4 paragraphs)

journal.prompt: If you could have dinner with Alexandre Dumas and ask him three questions about The Three Musketeers, what would you ask? Why? (1-2 paragraphs)
D'Artagnan left home to become a musketeer. Write about a time you tried something new or went somewhere for the first time. How did you feel? (1-2 paragraphs)
The musketeers were famous for their swordsmanship. What skill do you have or want to develop? Why is this skill important to you? (1-2 paragraphs)
Becoming a musketeer required years of training. Write about something difficult you've learned. What made it hard? What helped you keep trying? (1-2 paragraphs)
The royal court was full of secrets and intrigue. Describe a time when you discovered a secret or surprise. What happened? (1-2 paragraphs)
D'Artagnan was proud of being from Gascony. What are you proud of about where you come from or your family? (1-2 paragraphs)
People in the 1600s wrote letters instead of texting. If you could only communicate by letter for a week, how would your life change? (1-2 paragraphs)
The musketeers often met at taverns to eat and plan. Describe your favorite place to meet with friends or family. What makes it special? (1-2 paragraphs)
The Three Musketeers were based on real people. If someone wrote a story based on you and your friends, what would happen in it? (1-2 paragraphs)
Medical care was dangerous in the 1600s. What modern invention or discovery are you most grateful for? Why? (1-2 paragraphs)
England and France were old enemies who didn't trust each other. Write about a time you made friends with someone you thought you wouldn't like. (1-2 paragraphs)
The story's theme is friendship and loyalty. Who is the most loyal friend you have? What makes them a good friend? (1-2 paragraphs)
